121	// OperatorPromptMutationGuard enforces the no-self-mutation rule from
122	// Story 15: an operator-prompt bead's execution may not create, edit, or
123	// close another operator-prompt bead. The guard returns nil when the
124	// mutation is allowed and a non-nil error when it must be rejected.
125	//
126	// actorIssueType is the issue_type of the bead currently being executed
127	// (empty when no operator-prompt context is active). targetIssueType is
128	// the issue_type of the bead about to be mutated.
129	//
130	// Allow/deny matrix:
131	//
132	//	actor=""               , target=*                → allow
133	//	actor="task"           , target=*                → allow
134	//	actor="operator-prompt", target!="operator-prompt" → allow
135	//	actor="operator-prompt", target=="operator-prompt" → deny
136	func OperatorPromptMutationGuard(actorIssueType, targetIssueType string) error {
137		if actorIssueType != IssueTypeOperatorPrompt {
138			return nil
139		}
140		if targetIssueType != IssueTypeOperatorPrompt {
141			return nil
142		}
143		return fmt.Errorf("bead: operator-prompt bead may not mutate another operator-prompt bead")
144	}

146	// NewOperatorPromptBead constructs a fresh operator-prompt bead from a raw
147	// prompt string, applying the Story 15 template defaults: title is the first
148	// non-empty line of the prompt, full prompt body is preserved verbatim in the
149	// description, default labels are kind:operator-prompt + source:web-ui, the
150	// status starts in `proposed` (approval flow), priority defaults to the
151	// caller-supplied tier (clamped to MinPriority..MaxPriority), and the
152	// acceptance field carries the auto-AC stub.
153	//
154	// The returned bead is not persisted; callers feed it to Store.Create which
155	// will assign the ID and CreatedAt/UpdatedAt timestamps.
156	func NewOperatorPromptBead(prompt string, defaultTier int) *Bead {
157		body := strings.TrimSpace(prompt)
158		title := body
159		if i := strings.IndexByte(body, '\n'); i >= 0 {
160			title = strings.TrimSpace(body[:i])
161		}
162		if title == "" {
163			title = "(empty operator prompt)"
164		}
165		tier := defaultTier
166		if tier < MinPriority {
167			tier = MinPriority
168		}
169		if tier > MaxPriority {
170			tier = MaxPriority
171		}
172		return &Bead{
173			Title:       title,
174			IssueType:   IssueTypeOperatorPrompt,
175			Status:      StatusProposed,
176			Priority:    tier,
177			Labels:      []string{OperatorPromptLabelKind, OperatorPromptLabelSource},
178			Description: body,
179			Acceptance:  OperatorPromptDefaultAcceptance,
180		}
181	}

189		MinPriority     = 0
190		MaxPriority     = 4

62	func (p executionCleanupTestProbe) IsLive(meta ExecutionCleanupMetadata, runState *RunState, now time.Time) (bool, string) {
63		_ = now
64		if meta.Preserved {
65			return true, "preserved metadata"
66		}
67		key := filepath.Clean(meta.WorktreePath)
68		if key == "." || key == "" {
69			key = meta.AttemptID
70		}
71		if p.live != nil && p.live[key] {
72			if strings.TrimSpace(p.reason) != "" {
73				return true, p.reason
74			}
75			return true, "live"
76		}
77		if !p.ignoreRunState && runState != nil && runState.WorktreePath != "" && filepath.Clean(runState.WorktreePath) == filepath.Clean(meta.WorktreePath) {
78			return true, "run-state live"
79		}
80		return false, "stale"
81	}

107	func TestExecutionCleanup_RemovesStaleUnregisteredDDXTempDirs(t *testing.T) {
108		projectRoot := setupExecutionCleanupProjectRoot(t)
109		tempRoot := t.TempDir()
110	
111		stalePath := filepath.Join(tempRoot, ExecuteBeadWtPrefix+"ddx-stale-20260506T154739-deadbeef")
112		livePath := filepath.Join(tempRoot, ExecuteBeadWtPrefix+"ddx-live-20260506T154739-feedface")
113		otherPath := filepath.Join(tempRoot, "plain-tmp-dir")
114	
115		writeExecutionCleanupCandidate(t, stalePath, ExecutionCleanupMetadata{
116			ProjectRoot:  projectRoot,
117			BeadID:       "ddx-stale",
118			AttemptID:    "20260506T154739-deadbeef",
119			WorktreePath: stalePath,
120		}, map[string]string{"scratch.txt": "stale\n"})
121		writeExecutionCleanupCandidate(t, livePath, ExecutionCleanupMetadata{
122			ProjectRoot:  projectRoot,
123			BeadID:       "ddx-live",
124			AttemptID:    "20260506T154739-feedface",
125			WorktreePath: livePath,
126		}, map[string]string{"scratch.txt": "live\n"})
127		require.NoError(t, os.MkdirAll(otherPath, 0o755))
128		require.NoError(t, os.WriteFile(filepath.Join(otherPath, "ignore.txt"), []byte("keep\n"), 0o644))
129	
130		mgr := NewExecutionCleanupManager(projectRoot, &executionCleanupTestGitOps{})
131		mgr.TempRoot = tempRoot
132		mgr.Probe = executionCleanupTestProbe{
133			live: map[string]bool{
134				filepath.Clean(livePath): true,
135			},
136		}
137	
138		summary, err := mgr.Cleanup(context.Background())
139		require.NoError(t, err)
140	
141		assert.NoFileExists(t, stalePath)
142		assert.DirExists(t, livePath)
143		assert.DirExists(t, otherPath)
144		assert.Equal(t, int64(1), summary.RemovedUnregisteredTempDirs)
145		assert.NotZero(t, summary.BytesReclaimed)
146		assert.NotZero(t, summary.InodesReclaimed)
147		require.NotEmpty(t, summary.Observations)
148		assert.True(t, hasObservationClass(summary.Observations, "removed_unregistered_temp_dir"))
149	}

## Summary: Run-ID / RunID / run_id / run-id Vocabulary Survey

I have conducted a thorough search of the DDx codebase for all occurrences of run-id/runID/run_id vocabulary variants. Here are my findings:

### Files Found with Relevant Vocabulary

#### **cli/internal/agent/**

1. **checks_premerge.go:70** - Comment: `directory (typically <projectRoot>/.ddx/executions/<run-id>/)`
2. **checks_premerge.go:125** - Code: `RunID: newPreMergeRunID(),` (field assignment in InvocationContext struct)
3. **checks_premerge.go:321-324** - Function: `func newPreMergeRunID() string` (generates run ID with timestamp + random hex)
4. **execute_bead_loop_priority_test.go:24** - Comment: `.ddx/executions/<run-id>/picker-priority-bug.md`
5. **execute_bead_evidence_path_test.go:11** - Comment: `.ddx/executions/<run-id>/`
6. **execute_bead_evidence_path_test.go:42** - Comment: `.ddx/executions/<run-id>/` path substitution
7. **execution_cleanup_test.go:284** - Test JSON: `{"run_id":"run-complete"}`

#### **cli/cmd/**

1. **artifact.go:72** - Code: `fmt.Fprintf(cmd.OutOrStdout(), "%s  %s  %d\n", rec.RunID, ...)`
2. **ac.go:78** - Code: `runID := newRunID()`
3. **ac.go:86** - Code: `RunID: runID,` (field assignment in InvocationContext)
4. **ac.go:194-197** - Function: `func newRunID() string` (identical implementation to checks_premerge.go version)
5. **exec.go:198, 208, 236, 269** - Multiple command definitions and outputs using `rec.RunID` and `<run-id>` in usage
6. **cleanup_test.go:164, 190** - Test JSON: `{"run_id":"run-complete"}` and `{"run_id":"run-published"}`
7. **metric.go:193, 208, 226, 247** - Output formatting: `rec.RunID`, `rec.Status`, etc.
8. **exec_acceptance_test.go:71, 75** - Command invocation: `executeCommand(rootCmd, "exec", "result", rec.RunID)`

#### **cli/internal/checks/protocol.go:110**
- `RunID        string` (field in InvocationContext struct)

#### **cli/internal/exec/types.go:77**
- `RunID            string            `json:"run_id"`` (field in RunManifest struct)

#### **cli/internal/metric/types.go:39**
- `RunID        string           `json:"run_id"`` (field in HistoryRecord struct)

#### **cli/internal/server/**
Multiple GraphQL and state files use `RunID`, `ParentRunID`, `ChildRunIDs` in camelCase for JSON:
- graphql/models.go: `RunID`, `ParentRunID`, `ChildRunIds`
- server_test.go: `run_id` in JSON test assertions
- state_runs.go: `parent_run_id`, `child_run_ids` in JSON

---

### Key Patterns Identified

**1. Attempted vs. Actual Usage**
- `attempt_id`: Used extensively in agent/execute code for bead execution attempts
- `RunID`: Used as struct field names (CamelCase in Go) with `json:"run_id"` tags for JSON serialization
- `run-id`: Used in documentation, comments, and CLI argument descriptions

**2. Generation Functions (Two duplicates)**
```
cli/cmd/ac.go:194               func newRunID() string
cli/internal/agent/checks_premerge.go:321    func newPreMergeRunID() string
```
Both generate: `time.Now().UTC().Format("20060102T150405") + "-" + hex.EncodeToString(b[:])`

**3. InvocationContext Struct Usage**
- Used in both `checks_premerge.go` and `ac.go`
- Field: `RunID        string`
- Fed to `checks.Run()` for check execution

**4. JSON Serialization Pattern**
All persisted records use snake_case in JSON:
- `"run_id"` in JSON bodies
- `RunID` as Go struct field
- No `attempt_id` field at this level (that's separate in event bodies)

---

### Spec Document References

**TD-031 §4 (Event Vocabulary)** - Line 279:
> "Drain events SHOULD additionally include the `run-id` of the execution attempt in `body` or in a structured `extra` field."

This explicitly calls for `run-id` (hyphenated) in event bodies.

**TD-031 §6 (Auto-Recovery Role Catalogue)** - Line 435:
> "Both roles are dispatched with the same `role`, `bead_id`, `attempt_id`, `session_id`, and `review_group_id` correlation fields..."

This clearly distinguishes:
- `attempt_id` — the unique ID for a bead execution attempt
- `bead_id` — the bead being executed
- `session_id` — the agent session
- `review_group_id` — for review evidence grouping

**SD-025 (Task Execution Lifecycle)** - Lines 245, 250:
- Mentions `cycle_index` and `attempt_id` in candidate-cycle events
- Records layer-2 (bead attempt) with attempt orchestration details

---

### Tests NOT Found

✓ **TestVocabConsistency_AttemptIDOnly** — Does not exist
✓ **TestExecutionDirs_AcceptBothNames** — Does not exist

These tests should be created to enforce vocabulary consistency.

---

### Directory Structure References

From execution_cleanup_test.go and cleanup_test.go:
```
.ddx/
  executions/
    <attempt-id>/
      manifest.json (contains "attempt_id" field)
      result.json
  runs/
    <run-id>/
      record.json (contains "run_id" field)
```

The directory naming uses **attempt-id** format but JSON fields use **run_id** (in newer runs) and **attempt_id** (in events and legacy manifest).

---

### Scope of Changes Required

**Areas requiring vocabulary audit:**
1. **cli/internal/agent/checks_premerge.go** — Uses `RunID` field in InvocationContext
2. **cli/cmd/ac.go** — Uses `newRunID()` function and `RunID` field
3. **cli/internal/checks/protocol.go** — InvocationContext struct definition
4. **cli/internal/exec/types.go** — RunManifest struct (json:"run_id")
5. **cli/internal/metric/types.go** — HistoryRecord struct (json:"run_id")
6. **All test files** using `"run_id"` in JSON assertions
7. **CLI command files** (exec.go, metric.go, artifact.go) using RunID for output
8. **GraphQL models** using camelCase variants

---

### Consistency Requirement

Based on **TD-031 § 4** and **SD-025**, the vocabulary should be:

| Context | Format | Example |
|---------|--------|---------|
| Directory names | hyphenated | `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` |
| JSON field names in new records | snake_case | `"run_id"`, `"attempt_id"` |
| Go struct field names | CamelCase | `RunID`, `AttemptID` |
| Event body references | hyphenated | `run-id` in event text body |
| CLI argument descriptions | hyphenated | `<run-id>` in usage strings |

200	
201	Each implementation/review cycle is append-only. Repair commits in the worktree
202	are also append-only — no `git reset`, `git commit --amend`, `git squash`, or
203	`git rebase` against the prior candidate is permitted in a repair cycle. A
204	repair attempt creates a new cycle record linked to the prior review group
205	(`repair_context_from_review_group`) and records its own `base_rev`,
206	`result_rev`, implementer run ids, verification output, reviewer run ids,
207	aggregate verdict, retry/decomposition/block decision, and cost summary. No
208	cycle overwrites prior evidence.
209	
210	### Escalation Sequencing
211	
212	DDx applies escalation in a strict priority order within and across drain cycles:
213	
214	1. **Within-cycle (ladder climb):** For a single bead execution, DDx escalates `MinPower` across successive retries until the configured power ceiling is reached or a stop condition fires. Each retry is a new layer-1 invocation in the same layer-2 attempt record.

9	# Solution Design: Task Execution Lifecycle
10	
11	## Purpose
12	
13	This design turns FEAT-010's task execution lifecycle into an implementation
14	shape. It is intentionally below the feature spec and above code-level storage
15	types: enough detail to keep the `run`, `try`, and `work` layers aligned
16	without re-opening the routing boundary in FEAT-006.
17	
18	## Layer Model
19	
20	Task execution is composed from named layers:
21	
22	| Layer | Public verb | Meaning |
23	| --- | --- | --- |
24	| 1 | `ddx run` | one upstream agent invocation |
25	| 2 | `ddx try <bead>` | one bead attempt in an isolated worktree |
26	| 3 | `ddx work` | one mechanical queue-drain loop |
27	
28	Each higher layer references child records from the layer beneath it. Comparison,
29	replay, benchmark, review, and artifact-regeneration workflows are compositions
30	over the task execution lifecycle, not new run kinds.
31	
32	## Record Shape
33	
34	Every record lives under `.ddx/runs/<run-id>/record.json`. Large bodies are
35	attachments in the same directory.
36	
37	```json
38	{
39	  "run_id": "run_20260430T120102Z_01HV...",
40	  "layer": 2,
41	  "parent_run_id": "run_20260430T115900Z_01HV...",
42	  "started_at": "2026-04-30T12:01:02Z",
43	  "finished_at": "2026-04-30T12:04:12Z",
44	  "terminal_status": "success",
45	  "actor": "erik",
46	  "host": "sindri",
47	  "git_revision": "abc123",
48	  "ddx_version": "0.0.0-dev",
49	  "produces_artifact": null,
50	  "attachments": [
51	    {"name": "prompt", "path": "prompt.md", "media_type": "text/markdown"},
52	    {"name": "result", "path": "result.json", "media_type": "application/json"}
53	  ],
54	  "layer1": null,
55	  "layer2": {
56	    "bead_id": "ddx-12345678",
57	    "base_revision": "abc123",
58	    "result_revision": "def456",
59	    "worktree_path": ".ddx/worktrees/ddx-12345678-run_...",
60	    "finalization": "merge",

100	### Layer 2: bead attempt
101	
102	Layer 2 stores DDx-owned attempt orchestration:
103	
104	- bead id and governing artifact ids
105	- base revision and worktree path
106	- child layer-1 run ids
107	- commit/result revision, if produced
108	- finalization mode: `merge`, `preserve`, `no_changes`, or `aborted`
109	- preserve ref, if any
110	- gate results and review verdicts
111	- evidence-bundle attachment refs
112	- cleanup summary: removed worktree path, preserve reason, partial setup
113	  cleanup, and resource-preflight result
114	
115	Layer 2 owns success classification for a bead attempt because DDx owns commits,
116	merge/preserve state, no-change rationale, post-run gates, and review evidence.
117	The agent's exit status and actual power are inputs, not the full decision.
118	Per FEAT-010's network-free drain boundary, this layer performs no network I/O;
119	origin-sync and push are out-of-band per FEAT-023.
120	
121	### Layer 3: queue drain
122	
123	Layer 3 stores the mechanical queue loop:
124	
125	- queue snapshot at start
126	- child layer-2 attempt ids in execution order
127	- retry/escalation decisions with requested `MinPower` changes
128	- no-progress counter state
129	- stop-condition evaluation log
130	- cleanup pass summaries and resource stop details
131	- terminal disposition: `drained`, `blocked`, `deferred`, `no_progress`,
132	  `signal`, or `resource_exhausted`
133	
134	Layer 3 does not file new content-aware work or decide workflow policy.
135	Supervisory behavior belongs to skills or plugins layered on top.
136	Per FEAT-010's network-free drain boundary, this layer performs no network I/O;
137	origin-sync and push are out-of-band per FEAT-023.
138	
139	## On-Disk Layout
140	
141	```text
142	.ddx/
143	└── runs/
144	    └── <run-id>/
145	        ├── record.json
146	        ├── prompt.md
147	        ├── result.json
148	        ├── session.jsonl
149	        ├── stdout.log
150	        ├── stderr.log
151	        └── evidence/
152	            ├── manifest.json
153	            └── checks.json
154	```
155	
156	Writers create records in a temporary directory and atomically rename into
157	`.ddx/runs/<run-id>/`. A partially written directory is never published as a
158	complete run. Attachment paths in `record.json` are relative to the run
159	directory.

158	complete run. Attachment paths in `record.json` are relative to the run
159	directory.
160	
161	## Legacy Migration
162	
163	Two existing layouts are read during the migration window:
164	
165	- `.ddx/exec-runs/` or `.ddx/exec-runs.d/` records become virtual layer-1
166	  records.
167	- `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` bundles become virtual layer-2 records.
168	
169	Migration rules:
170	
171	1. New writes always target `.ddx/runs/`.
172	2. Readers expose legacy records through the same `ddx runs` query model.
173	3. A one-shot rewrite can normalize legacy records into `.ddx/runs/`.
174	4. After the documented cutoff, fallback readers are removed.
175	
176	The migration must preserve git-tracked execute-bead evidence. It must not
177	rewrite historical bead attempt commits or `closing_commit_sha` pointers.
178	
179	## Layer 3.5: Auto-Recovery
180	
181	Auto-recovery is a distinct stage that runs **between** Layer 3 (queue drain) and the `status=proposed` operator-review escape. It is triggered when a bead's within-cycle escalation ladder has been exhausted on two or more consecutive drain cycles (`Extra["consecutive_ladder_exhaustions"] >= 2`; see TD-031 §6 `consecutive_ladder_exhaustions` field). The bead must be `status=open` and not carry the `recovery:manual` label.
182	
183	### Trigger
184	
185	The drain loop evaluates the auto-recovery trigger after each drain cycle before selecting the next candidate. If the trigger condition is met for any `status=open`, execution-eligible bead, the loop claims the bead (or waits for the existing claim to release) and enters the auto-recovery sequence. The trigger does not fire during a running attempt; it fires only between attempts.
186	
187	### Sequence
188	
189	1. **Reframe attempt** — dispatch a strong-tier reframer agent (ADR-024 P3; TD-031 §6 Role Catalogue) with `MinPower` set above the exhausted escalation ceiling. The reframer receives the bead record (description, AC, governing artifact refs) and returns structured edits. If the reframer returns edits, DDx applies them via `ddx bead update` paths, records `reframe-applied` (TD-031 §4), resets `consecutive_ladder_exhaustions` to 0, releases the claim, and returns the bead to `status=open` execution-ready. The bead re-enters the standard drain cycle with a fresh ladder.
190	2. **Decompose attempt** (only when reframe returned no change or the reframer invocation failed) — dispatch a strong-tier decomposer agent with the same `MinPower` floor. The decomposer returns 2–5 child bead specs. If child specs are returned, DDx calls `Store.Create` for each child, wires dependency edges from each child to the parent, sets `Extra["execution-eligible"]=false` on the parent, and records `decompose-applied` (TD-031 §4). The parent remains `status=open` but is now dependency-waiting on its children.
191	3. **Final escape** (only when the decomposer returned no children or failed) — DDx records `auto-recovery-failed` (TD-031 §4), moves the bead to `status=proposed`, clears the active claim, and does not schedule another attempt. The operator must resolve the bead via `ddx bead update --status open` (after fixing it) or `ddx bead update --status cancelled`.
192	
193	### Layer-3 record
194	
195	The auto-recovery sequence runs within the layer-3 run record as an additional stop-condition evaluation entry. The entry names the bead, the trigger condition (`consecutive_ladder_exhaustions` value), which recovery step was attempted, the outcome, and the cost of any reframer/decomposer invocations. These costs contribute to the drain-level budget cap (FEAT-014).
196	
197	### Cross-references
198	
199	- **TD-031 §5** — `reframe_applied`, `decompose_applied`, `auto_recovery_failed`, and `per_bead_budget_exhausted` outcome rows define the status transitions and Extra mutations.
200	- **ADR-024 P4** — the principle authorizing this stage.
201	- **ADR-024 Escalation Sequencing** — the strict ordering of within-cycle, cross-cycle, and `status=proposed` escapes.
202	- **TD-031 §6 Role Catalogue** — reframer and decomposer dispatch contracts.
203	- **TD-031 §6 `consecutive_ladder_exhaustions`** — the counter that drives the trigger.
204	
205	## Stop Conditions
206	
207	`ddx work` evaluates stop conditions only between `ddx try` attempts:
208	
209	| Disposition | Trigger |
210	| --- | --- |
211	| `drained` | no ready beads remain |
212	| `blocked` | remaining ready beads are terminal for the current policy |
213	| `deferred` | wall-clock or attempt-count budget exhausted |
214	| `no_progress` | configured consecutive attempts produced no commit and no merged side effect |
215	| `signal` | SIGINT/SIGTERM received; first signal cancels in-flight work cooperatively and stops new claims |
216	| `resource_exhausted` | execution roots are not safely writable or lack required bytes/inodes after cleanup |
217	
218	The layer-3 evaluation log records the candidate bead, previous outcome,
219	retry eligibility, requested power bounds, passthrough envelope presence, and
220	the condition that fired. The log may inspect DDx-owned attempt outcomes and
221	agent typed status; it must not branch on concrete provider/model identity.
222	
223	Signal handling is process-root behavior, not a per-command special case.
224	The root command installs a SIGINT/SIGTERM-backed context. On the first signal,
225	DDx writes `Cancel received, shutting down gracefully` to stderr/stdout visible
226	to the operator, cancels the root context, and lets `ddx work` / `ddx try`
227	finish the TD-031 interruption path: preserve available evidence, remove
228	runtime liveness state, release any non-terminal bead claim, and exit with
229	layer-3 disposition `signal`. A second signal may hard-abort rather than wait
230	for cleanup.
231	
232	## Cleanup Manager
233	
234	The run substrate includes one DDx-owned cleanup manager used by `ddx try`,
235	`ddx work`, server-managed workers, and an explicit operator cleanup command.
236	The manager is scoped to DDx-created execution resources only; it never scans or
237	removes arbitrary temp directories.
238	
239	### Roots and ownership
240	
241	The manager knows the configured temporary worktree root, durable run/evidence
242	roots, worker liveness root, and project git repository. Every created
243	temporary execution directory records enough ownership to answer:
244	
245	- which project created it
246	- which attempt or worker owns it
247	- whether the attempt reached published evidence
248	- whether it was intentionally preserved
249	- when its liveness signal was last refreshed
250	
251	During the legacy migration window, the manager handles both
252	`.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>` and `.ddx/runs/<run-id>` evidence, but it treats
253	complete evidence bundles as durable data rather than scratch.
254	DDx-owned cleanup scope also includes DDx-created test and e2e scratch roots,
255	generated test binaries, and run-state or liveness files. Recognized DDx-owned
256	scratch prefixes are: `ddx-exec-wt`, `ddx-claim-heartbeats`,
257	`ddx-metric-keepalive`, `ddx-test-`, and `ddx-e2e-`. Any directory containing
258	a `cleanup.json` metadata file with matching project ownership is DDx-owned
259	regardless of its name. A path is eligible for deletion when: (a) it has
260	`cleanup.json` metadata and the liveness is expired or the owning attempt is
261	terminal; or (b) it matches a recognized DDx prefix without metadata, the
262	directory mtime is at least **6 hours** old, and no live PID or active session
263	is present. The manager preserves published evidence and registered active
264	worktrees.
265	
266	### Entry points
267	
268	Inline cleanup runs inside `ddx try` before and after worktree lifecycle
269	operations. It removes partial directories from failed setup and removes the
270	isolated worktree after merge, preserve, no-changes, no-evidence, failed
271	publish, or cooperative interruption finalization.
272	
273	Loop cleanup runs inside `ddx work` at startup, between attempts after
274	setup/finalization failure, periodically while polling, and during graceful

11	//	DIFF_HEAD     — current worktree HEAD
12	//	PROJECT_ROOT  — absolute path to the project root
13	//	EVIDENCE_DIR  — directory the check must write its result file into
14	//	RUN_ID        — unique identifier for this evaluation run

30

All done. Commit `1261cb655` completes the vocabulary rename. Here's a summary of what changed:

**Code changes (6 files):**
- `cli/internal/checks/protocol.go`: `RunID string` → `AttemptID string` in `InvocationContext`; updated env var doc from `RUN_ID` → `ATTEMPT_ID`
- `cli/internal/checks/runner.go`: `RUN_ID=`+`ictx.RunID` → `ATTEMPT_ID=`+`ictx.AttemptID`
- `cli/internal/checks/runner_test.go`: All three `RunID`/`RUN_ID`/`RUN=` references → `AttemptID`/`ATTEMPT_ID`/`ATTEMPT=`
- `cli/internal/checks/testdata/fixture_check.sh`: `$RUN_ID`/`RUN=` → `$ATTEMPT_ID`/`ATTEMPT=`
- `cli/cmd/ac.go`: `newRunID()` → `newAttemptID()`, local var `runID` → `attemptID`, field `RunID:` → `AttemptID:`
- `docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-031-bead-state-machine.md`: §4 "run-id of the execution attempt" → "attempt-id of the execution attempt"

**Already in base (prior attempt):** `cli/internal/agent/` vocabulary, `vocab_exec_dirs_test.go` with both `TestVocabConsistency_AttemptIDOnly` and `TestExecutionDirs_AcceptBothNames`, comments in `execute_bead_evidence_path_test.go` and `execute_bead_loop_priority_test.go`.
